name: pickle5

metadata.name: pickle5
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Python Software Foundation License
metadata.summary: Backport of the pickle 5 protocol (PEP 574) and other pickle changes

documentation:
# `pickle5` -- A backport of the pickle 5 protocol (PEP 574)

This package backports all features and APIs added in the `pickle` module
in Python 3.8.3, including the
[PEP 574](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0574/) additions. It should
work with Python 3.5, 3.6 and 3.7.

Basic usage is similar to the `pickle` module, except that the module
to be imported is `pickle5`:

```python
import pickle5 as pickle

pb = pickle.PickleBuffer(b"foo")
data = pickle.dumps(pb, protocol=5)
assert pickle.loads(data) == b"foo"
```

Detailed documentation can be found in PEP 574 and the standard
[pickle documentation](https://docs.python.org/3.8/library/pickle.html).

```{image} https://travis-ci.org/pitrou/pickle5-backport.svg?branch=master
:align: left
:target: https://travis-ci.org/pitrou/pickle5-backport
```

```{image} https://ci.appveyor.com/api/projects/status/hk4lrl81wp0q283o/branch/master?svg=true
:align: left
:target: https://ci.appveyor.com/project/pitrou/pickle5-backport/branch/master
```

modules.name: pickle5.pickletools
documentation:
"Executable documentation" for the pickle module.

Extensive comments about the pickle protocols and pickle-machine opcodes
can be found here.  Some functions meant for external use:

genops(pickle)
   Generate all the opcodes in a pickle, as (opcode, arg, position) triples.

dis(pickle, out=None, memo=None, indentlevel=4)
   Print a symbolic disassembly of a pickle.

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.read_uint1
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io
>>> read_uint1(io.BytesIO(b'\xff'))
255
```

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.read_uint2
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io
>>> read_uint2(io.BytesIO(b'\xff\x00'))
255
>>> read_uint2(io.BytesIO(b'\xff\xff'))
65535
```

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.read_int4
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io
>>> read_int4(io.BytesIO(b'\xff\x00\x00\x00'))
255
>>> read_int4(io.BytesIO(b'\x00\x00\x00\x80')) == -(2**31)
True
```

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.read_uint4
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io
>>> read_uint4(io.BytesIO(b'\xff\x00\x00\x00'))
255
>>> read_uint4(io.BytesIO(b'\x00\x00\x00\x80')) == 2**31

```

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.read_uint8
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io
>>> read_uint8(io.BytesIO(b'\xff\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00'))
255
>>> read_uint8(io.BytesIO(b'\xff' * 8)) == 2**64-1
True
```

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.read_stringnl
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io
>>> read_stringnl(io.BytesIO(b"'abcd'\nefg\n"))
'abcd'
```

```pycon
>>> read_stringnl(io.BytesIO(b"\n"))
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: no string quotes around b''
```

```pycon
>>> read_stringnl(io.BytesIO(b"\n"), stripquotes=False)
''
```

```pycon
>>> read_stringnl(io.BytesIO(b"''\n"))
''
```

```pycon
>>> read_stringnl(io.BytesIO(b'"abcd"'))
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: no newline found when trying to read stringnl
```

Embedded escapes are undone in the result.
\>>> read_stringnl(io.BytesIO(br"'an\\bx00ctd'" + b"n'e'"))
'an\\bx00ctd'

documentation:

```pycon
>>> import io
>>> read_stringnl_noescape_pair(io.BytesIO(b"Queue\nEmpty\njunk"))
'Queue Empty'
```

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.read_bytes8
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io, struct, sys
>>> read_bytes8(io.BytesIO(b"\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00abc"))
b''
>>> read_bytes8(io.BytesIO(b"\x03\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00abcdef"))
b'abc'
>>> bigsize8 = struct.pack("<Q", sys.maxsize//3)
>>> read_bytes8(io.BytesIO(bigsize8 + b"abcdef"))  #doctest: +ELLIPSIS
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: expected ... bytes in a bytes8, but only 6 remain
```

```pycon
>>> read_bytearray8(io.BytesIO(b"\x03\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00abcdef"))
```

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.read_unicodestringnl
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io
>>> read_unicodestringnl(io.BytesIO(b"abc\\uabcd\njunk")) == 'abc\uabcd'
True
```

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.read_unicodestring1
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io
>>> s = 'abcd\uabcd'
>>> enc = s.encode('utf-8')
>>> enc
b'abcd\xea\xaf\x8d'
>>> n = bytes([len(enc)])  # little-endian 1-byte length
>>> t = read_unicodestring1(io.BytesIO(n + enc + b'junk'))
>>> s == t
True
```

```pycon
>>> read_unicodestring1(io.BytesIO(n + enc[:-1]))
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: expected 7 bytes in a unicodestring1, but only 6 remain
```

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.read_unicodestring4
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io
>>> s = 'abcd\uabcd'
>>> enc = s.encode('utf-8')
>>> enc
b'abcd\xea\xaf\x8d'
>>> n = bytes([len(enc), 0, 0, 0])  # little-endian 4-byte length
>>> t = read_unicodestring4(io.BytesIO(n + enc + b'junk'))
>>> s == t
True
```

```pycon
>>> read_unicodestring4(io.BytesIO(n + enc[:-1]))
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: expected 7 bytes in a unicodestring4, but only 6 remain
```

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.read_unicodestring8
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io
>>> s = 'abcd\uabcd'
>>> enc = s.encode('utf-8')
>>> enc
b'abcd\xea\xaf\x8d'
>>> n = bytes([len(enc)]) + b'\0' * 7  # little-endian 8-byte length
>>> t = read_unicodestring8(io.BytesIO(n + enc + b'junk'))
>>> s == t
True
```

```pycon
>>> read_unicodestring8(io.BytesIO(n + enc[:-1]))
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: expected 7 bytes in a unicodestring8, but only 6 remain
```

```pycon
>>> read_long1(io.BytesIO(b"\x00\x00\x00\x00"))

```

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools._genops
documentation: A pickle opcode generator.

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.genops
documentation:
Generate all the opcodes in a pickle.

'pickle' is a file-like object, or string, containing the pickle.

Each opcode in the pickle is generated, from the current pickle position,
stopping after a STOP opcode is delivered.  A triple is generated for
each opcode:

    opcode, arg, pos

opcode is an OpcodeInfo record, describing the current opcode.

If the opcode has an argument embedded in the pickle, arg is its decoded
value, as a Python object.  If the opcode doesn't have an argument, arg
is None.

If the pickle has a tell() method, pos was the value of pickle.tell()
before reading the current opcode.  If the pickle is a bytes object,
it's wrapped in a BytesIO object, and the latter's tell() result is
used.  Else (the pickle doesn't have a tell(), and it's not obvious how
to query its current position) pos is None.

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.optimize
documentation: A pickle optimizer.

functions.name: pickle5.pickletools.dis
documentation:
Produce a symbolic disassembly of a pickle.

'pickle' is a file-like object, or string, containing a (at least one)
pickle.  The pickle is disassembled from the current position, through
the first STOP opcode encountered.

Optional arg 'out' is a file-like object to which the disassembly is
printed.  It defaults to sys.stdout.

Optional arg 'memo' is a Python dict, used as the pickle's memo.  It
may be mutated by dis(), if the pickle contains PUT or BINPUT opcodes.
Passing the same memo object to another dis() call then allows disassembly
to proceed across multiple pickles that were all created by the same
pickler with the same memo.  Ordinarily you don't need to worry about this.

Optional arg 'indentlevel' is the number of blanks by which to indent
a new MARK level.  It defaults to 4.

Optional arg 'annotate' if nonzero instructs dis() to add short
description of the opcode on each line of disassembled output.
The value given to 'annotate' must be an integer and is used as a
hint for the column where annotation should start.  The default
value is 0, meaning no annotations.

In addition to printing the disassembly, some sanity checks are made:

+ All embedded opcode arguments "make sense".

+ Explicit and implicit pop operations have enough items on the stack.

+ When an opcode implicitly refers to a markobject, a markobject is
  actually on the stack.

+ A memo entry isn't referenced before it's defined.

+ The markobject isn't stored in the memo.

+ A memo entry isn't redefined.
A symbolic pickle disassembler.

classes.name: pickle5.pickletools.StackObject
documentation: Object descriptors.  The stack used by the pickle machine holds objects,
and in the stack_before and stack_after attributes of OpcodeInfo
descriptors we need names to describe the various types of objects that can
appear on the stack.

classes.name: pickle5.pickletools.OpcodeInfo
documentation: Descriptors for pickle opcodes.

classes.name: pickle5.pickletools._Example
documentation: For use in the doctest, simply as an example of a class to pickle.

functions.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.opcode_in_pickle
documentation: Return True if opcode code appears in the pickle, else False.

functions.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.count_opcode
documentation: Return the number of times opcode code appears in pickle.

functions.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.setstate_bbb
documentation:
Custom state setter for BBB objects

Such callable may be created by other persons than the ones who created the
BBB class. If passed as the state_setter item of a custom reducer, this
allows for custom state setting behavior of BBB objects. One can think of
it as the analogous of list_setitems or dict_setitems but for foreign
classes/functions.

classes.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.MinimalIO
documentation: A file-like object that doesn't support readinto().

classes.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.ExtensionSaver

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.ExtensionSaver.__init__
documentation: Remember current registration for code (if any), and remove it (if
there is one).

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.ExtensionSaver.restore
documentation: Restore previous registration for code.
We can't very well test the extension registry without putting known stuff
in it, but we have to be careful to restore its original state.  Code
should do this:
e = ExtensionSaver(extension_code)
try:
fiddle w/ the extension registry's stuff for extension_code
finally:
e.restore()

classes.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.K
documentation: Hashable mutable key

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.AbstractUnpickleTests.assert_is_copy
documentation: Utility method to verify if two objects are copies of each others.

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.AbstractPickleTests.dont_test_disassembly
documentation: There are gratuitous differences between pickles produced by
pickle and cPickle, largely because cPickle starts PUT indices at
1 and pickle starts them at 0.  See XXX comment in cPickle's put2() --
there's a comment with an exclamation point there whose meaning
is a mystery.  cPickle also suppresses PUT for objects with a refcount
of 1.

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.AbstractPickleTests.test_proto
documentation: Tests for protocol 2

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.AbstractPickleTests.produce_global_ext
documentation: Register a type with copyreg, with extension code extcode.  Pickle
an object of that type.  Check that the resulting pickle uses opcode
(EXT[124]) under proto 2, and not in proto 1.

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.AbstractPickleTests.check_frame_opcodes
documentation:
Check the arguments of FRAME opcodes in a protocol 4+ pickle.

Note that binary objects that are larger than FRAME_SIZE_TARGET are not
framed by default and are therefore considered a frame by themselves in
the following consistency check.

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.AbstractPickleTests.buffer_like_objects
documentation: PEP 574 tests below

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.AbstractPickleTests.test_oob_buffers
documentation: XXX Unfortunately cannot test non-contiguous array
(see comment in PicklableNDArray.__reduce_ex__)

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.BigmemPickleTests.test_huge_long_32b
documentation: Binary protocols can serialize longs of up to 2 GiB-1

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.BigmemPickleTests.test_huge_bytes_32b
documentation: Protocol 3 can serialize up to 4 GiB-1 as a bytes object
(older protocols don't have a dedicated opcode for bytes and are
too inefficient)

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.BigmemPickleTests.test_huge_str_32b
documentation: All protocols use 1-byte per printable ASCII character; we add another
byte because the encoded form has to be copied into the internal buffer.

methods.name: pickle5.test.pickletester.BigmemPickleTests.test_huge_str_64b
documentation: BINUNICODE (protocols 1, 2 and 3) cannot carry more than 2**32 - 1 bytes
of utf-8 encoded unicode. BINUNICODE8 (protocol 4) supports these huge
unicode strings however.

modules.name: pickle5.pickle
documentation:
Create portable serialized representations of Python objects.

See module copyreg for a mechanism for registering custom picklers.
See module pickletools source for extensive comments.

Classes:

    Pickler
    Unpickler

Functions:

    dump(object, file)
    dumps(object) -> string
    load(file) -> object
    loads(string) -> object

Misc variables:

    __version__
    format_version
    compatible_formats

functions.name: pickle5.pickle._getattribute
documentation: Tools used for pickling.

functions.name: pickle5.pickle.whichmodule
documentation: Find the module an object belong to.

functions.name: pickle5.pickle.encode_long
documentation:
Encode a long to a two's complement little-endian binary string.
Note that 0 is a special case, returning an empty string, to save a
byte in the LONG1 pickling context.

```pycon
>>> encode_long(0)
b''
>>> encode_long(255)
b'\xff\x00'
>>> encode_long(32767)
b'\xff\x7f'
>>> encode_long(-256)
b'\x00\xff'
>>> encode_long(-32768)
b'\x00\x80'
>>> encode_long(-128)
b'\x80'
>>> encode_long(127)
b'\x7f'
>>>
```

functions.name: pickle5.pickle.decode_long
documentation:
Decode a long from a two's complement little-endian binary string.

```pycon
>>> decode_long(b'')
0
>>> decode_long(b"\xff\x00")
255
>>> decode_long(b"\xff\x7f")
32767
>>> decode_long(b"\x00\xff")
-256
>>> decode_long(b"\x00\x80")
-32768
>>> decode_long(b"\x80")
-128
>>> decode_long(b"\x7f")
127
```

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.PUT

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.BINFLOAT
documentation: push float; arg is 8-byte float encoding

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.TRUE
documentation: not an opcode; see INT docs in pickletools.py

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.FALSE
documentation: not an opcode; see INT docs in pickletools.py

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.PROTO
documentation: identify pickle protocol

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.NEWOBJ
documentation: build object by applying cls.__new__ to argtuple

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.EXT1
documentation: push object from extension registry; 1-byte index

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.EXT2
documentation: ditto, but 2-byte index

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.EXT4
documentation: ditto, but 4-byte index

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.TUPLE1
documentation: build 1-tuple from stack top

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.TUPLE2
documentation: build 2-tuple from two topmost stack items

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.TUPLE3
documentation: build 3-tuple from three topmost stack items

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.NEWTRUE
documentation: push True

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.NEWFALSE
documentation: push False

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.LONG1
documentation: push long from < 256 bytes

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.LONG4
documentation: push really big long

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.BINBYTES
documentation: push bytes; counted binary string argument

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.SHORT_BINUNICODE
documentation: push short string; UTF-8 length < 256 bytes

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.BINUNICODE8
documentation: push very long string

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.BINBYTES8
documentation: push very long bytes string

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.EMPTY_SET
documentation: push empty set on the stack

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.ADDITEMS
documentation: modify set by adding topmost stack items

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.FROZENSET
documentation: build frozenset from topmost stack items

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.NEWOBJ_EX
documentation: like NEWOBJ but work with keyword only arguments

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.STACK_GLOBAL
documentation: same as GLOBAL but using names on the stacks

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.MEMOIZE
documentation: store top of the stack in memo

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.FRAME
documentation: indicate the beginning of a new frame

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.BYTEARRAY8
documentation: push bytearray

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.NEXT_BUFFER
documentation: push next out-of-band buffer

variables.name: pickle5.pickle.READONLY_BUFFER
documentation: make top of stack readonly

classes.name: pickle5.pickle.PickleError
documentation: A common base class for the other pickling exceptions.

classes.name: pickle5.pickle.PicklingError
documentation:
This exception is raised when an unpicklable object is passed to the
dump() method.

classes.name: pickle5.pickle.UnpicklingError
documentation:
This exception is raised when there is a problem unpickling an object,
such as a security violation.

Note that other exceptions may also be raised during unpickling, including
(but not necessarily limited to) AttributeError, EOFError, ImportError,
and IndexError.

classes.name: pickle5.pickle._Stop
documentation: An instance of _Stop is raised by Unpickler.load_stop() in response to
the STOP opcode, passing the object that is the result of unpickling.

classes.name: pickle5.pickle._Pickler

methods.name: pickle5.pickle._Pickler.__init__
documentation:
This takes a binary file for writing a pickle data stream.

The optional *protocol* argument tells the pickler to use the
given protocol; supported protocols are 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5.
The default protocol is 4. It was introduced in Python 3.4, and
is incompatible with previous versions.

Specifying a negative protocol version selects the highest
protocol version supported.  The higher the protocol used, the
more recent the version of Python needed to read the pickle
produced.

The *file* argument must have a write() method that accepts a
single bytes argument. It can thus be a file object opened for
binary writing, an io.BytesIO instance, or any other custom
object that meets this interface.

If *fix_imports* is True and *protocol* is less than 3, pickle
will try to map the new Python 3 names to the old module names
used in Python 2, so that the pickle data stream is readable
with Python 2.

If *buffer_callback* is None (the default), buffer views are
serialized into *file* as part of the pickle stream.

If *buffer_callback* is not None, then it can be called any number
of times with a buffer view.  If the callback returns a false value
(such as None), the given buffer is out-of-band; otherwise the
buffer is serialized in-band, i.e. inside the pickle stream.

It is an error if *buffer_callback* is not None and *protocol*
is None or smaller than 5.

methods.name: pickle5.pickle._Pickler.clear_memo
documentation:
Clears the pickler's "memo".

The memo is the data structure that remembers which objects the
pickler has already seen, so that shared or recursive objects
are pickled by reference and not by value.  This method is
useful when re-using picklers.

methods.name: pickle5.pickle._Pickler.dump
documentation: Write a pickled representation of obj to the open file.

methods.name: pickle5.pickle._Pickler.memoize
documentation: Store an object in the memo.

methods.name: pickle5.pickle._Pickler.put
documentation: Return a PUT (BINPUT, LONG_BINPUT) opcode string, with argument i.

methods.name: pickle5.pickle._Pickler.get
documentation: Return a GET (BINGET, LONG_BINGET) opcode string, with argument i.
Pickling machinery

classes.name: pickle5.pickle._Unpickler

methods.name: pickle5.pickle._Unpickler.__init__
documentation:
This takes a binary file for reading a pickle data stream.

The protocol version of the pickle is detected automatically, so
no proto argument is needed.

The argument *file* must have two methods, a read() method that
takes an integer argument, and a readline() method that requires
no arguments.  Both methods should return bytes.  Thus *file*
can be a binary file object opened for reading, an io.BytesIO
object, or any other custom object that meets this interface.

The file-like object must have two methods, a read() method
that takes an integer argument, and a readline() method that
requires no arguments.  Both methods should return bytes.
Thus file-like object can be a binary file object opened for
reading, a BytesIO object, or any other custom object that
meets this interface.

If *buffers* is not None, it should be an iterable of buffer-enabled
objects that is consumed each time the pickle stream references
an out-of-band buffer view.  Such buffers have been given in order
to the *buffer_callback* of a Pickler object.

If *buffers* is None (the default), then the buffers are taken
from the pickle stream, assuming they are serialized there.
It is an error for *buffers* to be None if the pickle stream
was produced with a non-None *buffer_callback*.

Other optional arguments are *fix_imports*, *encoding* and
*errors*, which are used to control compatibility support for
pickle stream generated by Python 2.  If *fix_imports* is True,
pickle will try to map the old Python 2 names to the new names
used in Python 3.  The *encoding* and *errors* tell pickle how
to decode 8-bit string instances pickled by Python 2; these
default to 'ASCII' and 'strict', respectively. *encoding* can be
'bytes' to read theses 8-bit string instances as bytes objects.

methods.name: pickle5.pickle._Unpickler.load
documentation:
Read a pickled object representation from the open file.

Return the reconstituted object hierarchy specified in the file.

methods.name: pickle5.pickle._Unpickler.pop_mark
documentation: Return a list of items pushed in the stack after last MARK instruction.

methods.name: pickle5.pickle._Unpickler._instantiate
documentation: INST and OBJ differ only in how they get a class object.  It's not
only sensible to do the rest in a common routine, the two routines
previously diverged and grew different bugs.
klass is the class to instantiate, and k points to the topmost mark
object, following which are the arguments for klass.__init__.
Unpickling machinery
